threats.impact_description: Node.js event-loop saturation causing full application unavailability for all users for the duration of the attack, with no authentication required beyond a single forged JWT.
threats.controls_in_place: 2000ms vm execution timeout per request; JWT authentication required
threats.mitigation_title: Add express-rate-limit middleware to POST /b2b/v2/orders

steps: Import and configure `express-rate-limit` for the B2B endpoint with a conservative window and max: e.g. 10 requests per minute per IP.
Register the rate limiter middleware before the `b2bOrder()` handler at server.ts:645.
Consider adding a per-JWT-subject rate limit (using `keyGenerator: (req) => req.auth?.sub ?? req.ip`) to prevent distributed DoS from a single forged identity across multiple IPs.

threats.title: Unbounded Login Brute Force — No Rate Limit (server.ts:594)
threats.scenario: `server.ts:594` registers `app.post('/rest/user/login', login())` with no rate-limiting middleware. The password reset route has a rate limit (`server.ts:343`) but `/rest/user/login` does not. An attacker can fire unlimited login attempts against any account. The MD5 password hashing (see express-backend-010) means an offline pre-computed list suffices for common passwords. Additionally the rate-limit on `/rest/user/reset-password` uses `headers['X-Forwarded-For'] ?? ip` as the key (server.ts:346), which `app.enable('trust proxy')` at line 342 combined with the spoofable header allows any attacker to bypass by rotating the header value.
threats.evidence_summary: `server.ts:594` registers the login route without any `rateLimit()` middleware; `server.ts:342-347` shows the reset-password rate limit is bypassable via `X-Forwarded-For`.
threats.impact_description: Attacker brute-forces any user account without throttling; password-reset brute force is similarly unthrottled via header spoofing.
threats.controls_in_place: Rate limit on /rest/user/reset-password only (bypassable via X-Forwarded-For header spoofing).
threats.mitigation_title: Add rate limiting to /rest/user/login with IP-only key and account lockout

steps: Add `rateLimit({ windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, max: 20, keyGenerator: (req) => req.ip })` middleware before `login()` at `server.ts:594`.
Fix the reset-password rate limiter key to use `req.ip` only — remove the `headers['X-Forwarded-For']` lookup, or if a trusted proxy is required, validate the header against a known proxy allowlist.
Implement account lockout (e.g. increment a `loginAttempts` counter in the Users table; block after 10 failures for 15 minutes).

threats.title: XML/YAML Bomb DoS via Unauthenticated Upload Triggers Billion-Laughs Entity Expansion (routes/fileUpload.ts:83)
threats.affected_parameter: uploaded XML or YAML file body
threats.scenario: At fileUpload.ts:83, XML is parsed with `noent: true` inside a vm sandbox with a 2-second timeout. However, libxmljs2's entity expansion occurs at the C library (libxml2) level, before and outside the JavaScript execution context that the vm timeout guards. A billion-laughs XML attack (`<!DOCTYPE bomb [ <!ENTITY a "lol"> <!ENTITY b "&a;&a;&a;&a;&a;"> <!ENTITY c "&b;&b;&b;&b;&b;"> ... ]>`) causes exponential memory allocation in the native library, potentially exhausting the Node.js heap before the VM timer fires. Because the endpoint requires no authentication (finding file-upload-service-001), an attacker can submit concurrent requests to amplify resource exhaustion. The `xxeDosChallenge` at lines 91-93 confirms this vector is intentional but the authentication gap makes it exploitable at scale.
threats.evidence_summary: fileUpload.ts:83 passes `noent: true` to libxmljs2's C-level parser; the vm timeout at the same line guards only JavaScript execution, not C-level entity expansion memory allocation.
threats.impact_description: An unauthenticated attacker submitting concurrent billion-laughs payloads can exhaust Node.js heap memory, crashing the application and causing a denial of service for all users.
threats.controls_in_place: vm.createContext timeout (2000ms) — guards JS execution only, not C-level libxml entity expansion; multer 200KB size limit reduces the bomb payload window but not fully
threats.mitigation_title: Disable noent:true in libxmljs2 and add rate limiting on POST /file-upload to mitigate XML/YAML bomb DoS

steps: Set `noent: false` and `dtdload: false` in the libxmljs2 parseXml options (fileUpload.ts:83) — entity expansion cannot occur without DTD processing, eliminating billion-laughs attacks.
Add `rateLimit({ windowMs: 60 * 1000, max: 5 })` middleware before `handleXmlUpload` in the POST /file-upload chain at server.ts:309 to limit upload frequency per IP.
Apply the same rate limit to `/profile/image/url` (server.ts:311) to prevent SSRF-amplified fetch floods.

threats.title: Unbounded WebSocket Connection Acceptance Without Rate Limiting (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:19)
threats.scenario: The Socket.IO server created at line 19-20 accepts connections from any client with no connection rate limiting, no per-IP limit, and no maximum concurrent connection count. An attacker can open thousands of simultaneous WebSocket connections from a single host or distributed botnet. Each connection triggers the notification replay loop (lines 30-32), consuming CPU and memory proportional to the notifications array size. Socket.IO maintains per-connection state (socket object, event listener registrations) that accumulates unbounded. Combined with the absence of authentication, this attack requires no credentials.
threats.evidence_summary: `new Server(server, { cors: { origin: 'http://localhost:4200' } })` at line 20 creates the Socket.IO server with no `maxHttpBufferSize`, no connection limit, and no rate-limiting option configured.
threats.impact_description: An attacker exhausts server file descriptors, memory, and CPU, causing a denial of service for all legitimate WebSocket and HTTP users.
threats.controls_in_place: None identified — no connection limit, no rate limiting middleware.
threats.mitigation_title: Enforce connection rate limits and maximum concurrent WebSocket connections in registerWebsocketEvents.ts

steps: Configure Socket.IO `maxHttpBufferSize` option to cap incoming message size (e.g., `new Server(server, { maxHttpBufferSize: 1e4 })` for 10KB) to limit per-message memory consumption.

Implement a per-IP connection rate limiter using `socket.handshake.address` in the `io.use()` middleware: track connection counts per IP in a Map or Redis and reject if over threshold (e.g., 10 connections/IP).
Set a maximum message rate per socket using a token-bucket limiter on incoming events to prevent event flooding on existing connections.
Consider wrapping the Socket.IO endpoint behind the same Express rate-limiting middleware used for HTTP routes (e.g., `express-rate-limit` applied to `/socket.io/`).

threats.title: Client-Side Route Guards Bypassable Without Server Authorization (frontend/src/app/app.guard.ts:17)
threats.scenario: Angular's `LoginGuard.canActivate()` at app.guard.ts:17-24 checks only `localStorage.getItem('token')` truthiness, and `AdminGuard.canActivate()` at line 52-59 decodes the JWT with `jwtDecode` and inspects the `role` claim client-side. These guards control navigation to `/administration` and `/accounting` but can be bypassed using browser devtools or by manipulating the Angular router programmatically: `(1) AngularRouter.navigate(['/administration'])` triggered from the browser console; `(2) localStorage.setItem('token', '<any string>')` to pass the LoginGuard truthiness check; `(3) a crafted `alg:none` JWT with `{data:{role:'admin'}}` payload to pass AdminGuard decoding`. While the actual administration API endpoints should enforce server-side authorization, the absence of guard hardening allows the admin UI to load and expose its structure to unauthenticated actors.
threats.evidence_summary: `LoginGuard` returns `true` based solely on `localStorage.getItem('token')` being truthy — any non-empty string passes. `AdminGuard` trusts the client-side decoded JWT role claim without signature verification.
threats.impact_description: An attacker can load the admin UI without valid credentials, potentially exposing admin functionality whose server-side enforcement is inconsistent or absent.
threats.controls_in_place: Server-side API authorization should independently enforce permissions, but the guard bypass exposes admin UI surface.
threats.mitigation_title: Add server-side session validation endpoint called by guards before route activation

steps: Add a `/rest/user/whoami` or session-check API call in `LoginGuard.canActivate()` that returns 401 on invalid/expired tokens, blocking navigation on server-confirmed failure.
For `AdminGuard`, verify role server-side — the guard should call a protected endpoint that validates the JWT signature and role server-side before returning 200.
Remove the bare `localStorage.getItem('token')` truthiness check; replace with a reactive observable from the user service that reflects server-confirmed auth state.

threats.title: JWT Role Claims Not Validated Against Database at Request Time
threats.scenario: The `isAccounting()` middleware at lib/insecurity.ts:156 and `isDeluxe()` at line 167 check `decodedToken?.data?.role` from the JWT payload without performing a database lookup to confirm the user's current role. A user who was granted `accounting` or `admin` role and subsequently had that role revoked retains elevated access until their 6-hour JWT expires. More critically, with the hardcoded private key (auth-002) an attacker forges a token payload with `role: 'admin'`, signs it, and all role checks will pass — there is no server-side role truth to compare against.
threats.evidence_summary: lib/insecurity.ts:159 checks `decodedToken?.data?.role === roles.accounting` with no DB read — the token claim is treated as authoritative for access control decisions.
threats.impact_description: Role escalation via token forgery (facilitated by auth-002) or delayed revocation — an attacker or a revoked privileged user retains admin/accounting access for up to 6 hours after a privilege change.
threats.controls_in_place: JWT expiry at 6 hours provides eventual revocation.
threats.mitigation_title: Validate user role against database on privileged endpoint access rather than trusting JWT role claim alone

steps: In `isAccounting()`, after decoding the JWT, perform `UserModel.findByPk(decodedToken.data.id, { attributes: ['role'] })` and check the DB role rather than (or in addition to) the token claim.
Reduce JWT expiry from 6 hours to 15-30 minutes; issue refresh tokens to maintain session without prolonged privilege persistence.
Implement a token revocation list (blocklist in Redis/memory) so that role changes take effect immediately without waiting for token expiry.

threats.title: Two-Factor Authentication Disable via MD5 Password Preimage
threats.scenario: The 2FA disable handler at routes/2fa.ts:158 verifies the user's password with `user.password !== security.hash(password)` — where `security.hash()` is MD5 (lib/insecurity.ts:43). An attacker who obtains the MD5 password hash (e.g., via the SQL injection at auth-001, or from the `/rest/user/authentication-details` endpoint accessible to admin users) can compute a preimage using rainbow tables (CrackStation, hashcat) or trivially crack `admin123` (32-char MD5: `0192023a7bbd73250516f069df18b500`). With the preimage in hand, the attacker submits the cleartext password to disable 2FA, then completes account takeover without needing the TOTP token.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/2fa.ts:158 calls `security.hash(password)` which maps to `crypto.createHash('md5')` at lib/insecurity.ts:43 — weak hash used to gate 2FA disable operation.
threats.impact_description: Attacker who knows a user's MD5 hash (trivially obtained via SQLi) can disable 2FA on their account, eliminating the second authentication factor before completing takeover.
threats.controls_in_place: 2FA token required for initial login; disable requires password confirmation (but using MD5).
threats.mitigation_title: Migrate 2FA password confirmation to use bcrypt comparison consistent with auth-005 fix

steps: After implementing bcrypt in auth-005, update routes/2fa.ts setup() and disable() to use `await bcrypt.compare(password, user.password)` instead of `user.password !== security.hash(password)`.
Consider requiring the current TOTP token in addition to the password when disabling 2FA — defense in depth against credential-only attacks.
Add rate limiting to the 2FA disable endpoint.

threats.title: Sensitive Routes Registered Without Authentication Middleware
threats.scenario: State-changing operations on sensitive resources MUST require a proven session. A registration line that lacks any auth marker either trusts the URL itself or relies on a downstream check that the static signature cannot prove exists.
threats.mitigation_title: Add an explicit auth middleware (isAuthorized() / passport.authenticate() / requireAuth) to the route registration, or mark the route intentionally public via app.get(...) for read-only access only.

threats.title: Unauthenticated Admin Endpoints (server.ts:604)
threats.scenario: `server.ts:604-605` registers `GET /rest/admin/application-version` and `GET /rest/admin/application-configuration` without any `security.isAuthorized()` middleware before the handlers. These endpoints expose the full application configuration object (returned by `retrieveAppConfiguration()`) and the running version string to unauthenticated callers. The configuration object includes challenge-solving flags, customization parameters, and potentially any config values that include secrets or internal hostnames depending on the runtime config file. Additionally, `GET /rest/chatbot/status` and `POST /rest/chatbot/respond` (server.ts:629-630) have no auth guard, exposing the chatbot interaction surface unauthenticated.
threats.evidence_summary: `server.ts:604-605` and `629-630` register admin and chatbot REST handlers without preceding `security.isAuthorized()` middleware.
threats.impact_description: Unauthenticated callers read full application configuration; chatbot responds to unauthenticated requests, potentially leaking internal information or being abused for denial-of-service against the LLM backend.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no auth middleware on these routes.
threats.mitigation_title: Add isAuthorized() and admin role check to /rest/admin/* endpoints in server.ts

steps: Prefix `GET /rest/admin/application-version` and `GET /rest/admin/application-configuration` with `security.isAuthorized(), security.isAccounting()` (or a new `isAdmin()` guard) at `server.ts:604-605`.
Add `security.isAuthorized()` before the chatbot routes at `server.ts:629-630` if the chatbot is intended for authenticated users only.
Add a broad catch-all: `app.use('/rest/admin', security.isAuthorized(), isAdminMiddleware)` before all admin-prefix handlers.

threats.title: DOM XSS via Last-Login IP from JWT Payload (frontend/src/app/last-login-ip/last-login-ip.component.ts:39)
threats.scenario: `last-login-ip.component.ts:39` executes `this.sanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml('${payload.data.lastLoginIp}')` where `payload.data.lastLoginIp` is taken directly from the decoded JWT. If the server stores an attacker-controlled value as the `lastLoginIp` claim (e.g., by routing a login request through an HTTP client with a crafted `X-Forwarded-For: ` header), the payload executes as HTML in the Last Login IP display page. This cross-component chain — attacker-controlled header → server-stored in JWT → client renders as HTML — bypasses Angular's sanitization entirely.
threats.evidence_summary: `bypassSecurityTrustHtml` wraps a JWT claim interpolated directly into an HTML string, creating an XSS sink for any attacker-controlled data stored server-side in the JWT.
threats.impact_description: Attacker-controlled data in the `lastLoginIp` JWT field executes as script in the authenticated user's browser context, enabling session takeover or credential theft.
threats.controls_in_place: None — explicit sanitization bypass.
threats.mitigation_title: Render last-login IP as plain text, not trusted HTML

steps: Remove `bypassSecurityTrustHtml` at last-login-ip.component.ts:39. Assign `this.lastLoginIp = payload.data.lastLoginIp` as a plain string.
Update the template to use `{{lastLoginIp}}` interpolation instead of `[innerHTML]`, so Angular's auto-escaping applies.
Validate the `lastLoginIp` field server-side before storing it in the JWT — accept only valid IPv4/IPv6 patterns.

threats.title: No Authentication Event Audit Logging
threats.scenario: Neither the login handler (routes/login.ts) nor the password reset handler (routes/resetPassword.ts) emit structured audit log entries for authentication events. Successful logins, failed login attempts, password reset completions, and 2FA changes produce no immutable record. The `saveLoginIp` route (routes/saveLoginIp.ts:32) overwrites a single mutable `lastLoginIp` DB field — providing no history and no timestamp. An attacker who successfully exploits SQL injection (auth-001) or account takeover via guessed security answers leaves no forensic trace. Incident response teams cannot determine when, from where, or by whom unauthorized access occurred.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/login.ts afterLogin() at line 19 issues JWT and returns a response with no `logger.info()`, `console.log()`, or audit sink call; routes/resetPassword.ts line 44 updates the password with no audit record.
threats.impact_description: Compromised accounts cannot be detected through log analysis; forensic investigation is impossible; regulatory audit requirements (e.g., PCI-DSS Req 10) cannot be met.
threats.controls_in_place: None — only mutable lastLoginIp field, no log records.
threats.mitigation_title: Add structured audit logging for all authentication lifecycle events

steps: Integrate a structured logging library (e.g., `winston` with a JSON transport) and emit audit events on: successful login (user ID, IP, timestamp), failed login (email attempted, IP, timestamp), password reset (user ID, IP, timestamp), 2FA enable/disable (user ID, IP, timestamp).
Write audit logs to an append-only sink (separate log file or remote SIEM) — not the same mutable DB that the app writes session data to.
Include at minimum: `actor`, `action`, `resource`, `timestamp`, `sourceIp`, `outcome` in every audit record.
remediation.code_example:
// In afterLogin(), after JWT issuance:
auditLogger.info({ actor: user.data.email, action: 'LOGIN_SUCCESS', sourceIp: req.ip, timestamp: new Date().toISOString() })

threats.title: Missing Structured Audit Log for B2B Order Execution Events
threats.scenario: The B2B order handler at routes/b2bOrder.ts records no structured security event when an order is accepted or when a sandbox execution error occurs. The only logging in place is HTTP-level access logging via `morgan('combined')` at server.ts:338, which captures the HTTP method and URL but not the authenticated actor's identity from the JWT, the contents of `orderLinesData`, the order number assigned, or whether a sandbox error occurred. A B2B customer can therefore deny placing a particular order, and the operator has no cryptographically-bound, actor-attributed audit trail to prove otherwise. Post-incident forensic reconstruction of RCE attempts is also severely hampered.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/b2bOrder.ts contains no import of a logger and no structured log call; the sole logging path is morgan at server.ts:338 which emits HTTP combined-format lines without actor identity or payload.
threats.impact_description: Inability to attribute specific order-processing events or RCE attempts to a particular B2B actor, preventing fraud investigation and regulatory compliance for financial transactions.
threats.controls_in_place: HTTP access log via morgan (method, URL, status, user-agent only)
threats.mitigation_title: Add structured audit logging of actor identity, orderLinesData hash, and outcome to routes/b2bOrder.ts

steps: In routes/b2bOrder.ts, import the application's logger (e.g. `winston`) and emit a structured log entry on each order request including: JWT subject (`req.auth?.sub`), order number, SHA-256 hash of `orderLinesData` (not raw payload — avoid logging potentially malicious expressions), HTTP status, and timestamp.
Log sandbox errors with the error class/message at WARN level, including the actor subject and a truncated/hashed payload, so RCE attempts are detectable in log analysis.
Route audit logs to an append-only sink (e.g. a dedicated log group, Splunk, or ELK) separate from the application process to prevent log tampering by an attacker who achieves RCE.

threats.title: No Audit Logging for Data Write Operations — models/index.ts:39
threats.scenario: Sequelize is initialized in models/index.ts:30-40 with `logging: false`, suppressing all SQL query logging. MarsDB (data/mongodb.ts) has no logging configuration. Write operations to sensitive tables — including Users (role changes, account creation), Orders, and product reviews — produce no structured audit record linking the actor, action, affected resource, and timestamp. If an attacker exploits the mass assignment vulnerability (data-layer-003) to elevate their role to admin, or forges a product review via the NoSQL injection (data-layer-004), there is no log entry to detect or reconstruct the event. SOC2 CC7.2 requires that security events be identified and logged.
threats.evidence_summary: models/index.ts:39 sets `logging: false` in the Sequelize constructor options; data/mongodb.ts has no event hooks or logging configured for MarsDB collections.
threats.impact_description: Security-relevant write operations (role changes, account creation, review manipulation) leave no audit trail, making incident detection and forensic reconstruction impossible.
threats.controls_in_place: HTTP access logs are available via /support/logs (server.ts:281), but these are request-level logs without actor-to-action-to-resource resolution.
threats.mitigation_title: Enable structured audit logging for all write operations on sensitive models

steps: Add Sequelize lifecycle hooks (afterCreate, afterUpdate, afterDestroy) on UserModel to emit a structured audit log entry: `{ ts, actor: userId, action: 'create'|'update'|'delete', model: 'User', recordId, changedFields }`.
Write audit logs to an append-only sink (e.g. a separate audit table with no UPDATE/DELETE permissions, or a write-once log stream).
Add MarsDB update/insert hooks in data/mongodb.ts to log review and order write operations.
Change `logging: false` to a structured logger (e.g. `logging: (sql, timing) => auditLogger.info({ sql, timing })`) or keep false but ensure the hooks above cover security-relevant operations.

threats.title: No Audit Log on Unauthenticated File Upload or URL-Fetch Operations (server.ts:309-311)
threats.scenario: The POST /file-upload endpoint (server.ts:309) processes XML, ZIP, and YAML payloads but emits no structured audit log entry recording the caller's IP, timestamp, file name, file hash, or upload result. The `profileImageUrlUpload` handler (profileImageUrlUpload.ts:37) logs only on error via `logger.warn()`, with no structured record of successful URL fetches or the identity of the initiating user. If an XXE or SSRF attack is conducted and the attacker erases or rotates the server before detection, there is no tamper-evident record to reconstruct the attack timeline. PCI-DSS Requirement 10 mandates audit trails for all access to cardholder data systems; SOC2 CC7.2 requires logging of security events. Both apply because this component processes user-supplied files in a Tier 1 system.
threats.evidence_summary: No `logger.info()` or structured audit call exists in fileUpload.ts or the success path of profileImageUrlUpload.ts; the only logging is `logger.warn()` on exception.
threats.impact_description: Security incidents triggered through the file upload surface (XXE, SSRF, zip-slip) cannot be attributed to specific actors or reconstructed for forensic investigation, violating PCI-DSS Req 10 and SOC2 CC7.2.
threats.controls_in_place: Express access log middleware (if configured) captures HTTP request method and path but not file names, file hashes, or identity-bound context
threats.mitigation_title: Add structured audit log entries for all file upload and URL-fetch events including caller identity, filename, and outcome in fileUpload.ts and profileImageUrlUpload.ts

steps: Add `logger.info({ event: 'file_upload', userId: loggedInUser?.data?.id ?? 'anonymous', filename: file.originalname, size: file.size, outcome: 'accepted' })` at the successful-processing point in each upload handler.
For profileImageUrlUpload, log `{ event: 'url_fetch', userId: loggedInUser.data.id, url: url, outcome: 'success'|'error' }` in both success and error branches.
Pipe logs to an append-only sink (e.g., CloudWatch Logs, Splunk) to prevent tampering after an incident.
Configure log retention per PCI-DSS Req 10.5 (minimum 12 months, 3 months immediately available).

threats.title: Sandbox Error Details Leaked to B2B API Callers
threats.scenario: When `safeEval(orderLinesData)` throws an error that is not a timeout (lines 25-33 of b2bOrder.ts), the raw error object is passed to `next(err)`. Express's default error handler (or any generic error middleware) will serialize this error — potentially including the error class name, message text from within the notevil evaluator, and stack frames — and return it in the HTTP response body. An attacker probing for sandbox escape vectors can use this error oracle to fingerprint the evaluator version, understand which expression constructs are rejected, and refine their escape payload iteratively.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/b2bOrder.ts line 32 calls `next(err)` with the raw caught error from `vm.runInContext`, with no sanitization before forwarding to Express error middleware.
threats.impact_description: Disclosure of internal evaluator error messages and stack frames to API callers, enabling fingerprinting of the notevil version and iterative sandbox-escape payload refinement.
threats.controls_in_place: `utils.getErrorMessage(err)` used for string matching (line 26) but the raw `err` object is forwarded to next() on line 32
threats.mitigation_title: Sanitize sandbox errors before forwarding to Express error middleware in b2bOrder.ts

steps: In the catch block at routes/b2bOrder.ts:32, replace `next(err)` with `next(new Error('Invalid order expression'))` — a generic error message that reveals nothing about the internal evaluator.
Log the original error server-side (with the actor identity and payload hash) before discarding it from the response path.

Ensure the Express error-handling middleware at the application level also strips stack traces from 500 responses sent to clients in production (set `NODE_ENV=production` or use `helmet()` error filtering).

threats.title: Container images published without signing or provenance attestation
threats.scenario: The release pipeline pushes multi-platform Docker images to DockerHub for every tagged release but includes no cosign signing step and no SLSA provenance attestation. Downstream users cannot verify the published image originated from this repository's CI pipeline rather than a compromised registry intermediary.
threats.mitigation_title: Add cosign signing step to release workflow or use actions/attest-build-provenance

threats.title: Untrusted npm Install/Postinstall Scripts Enabled
threats.scenario: The production npm install in the Dockerfile does not use --ignore-scripts. Any compromised transitive dependency can execute arbitrary postinstall code during the Docker build phase, injecting backdoors or exfiltrating build secrets.
threats.mitigation_title: Use `npm ci --ignore-scripts` and audit required postinstalls separately

threats.title: Dependabot Ecosystem Coverage Incomplete
threats.scenario: No Dependabot configuration file exists. The npm dependency graph (450+ packages) receives no automated security update alerts. Known-vulnerable packages persist indefinitely until manually identified and updated.
threats.mitigation_title: Create .github/dependabot.yml with npm, github-actions, and docker ecosystem entries

threats.title: ReDoS via Unanchored Regex on Attacker-Controlled WebSocket Data (lib/startup/registerWebsocketEvents.ts:47)
threats.scenario: The `verifySvgInjectionChallenge` handler at line 46-47 calls `data?.match(/.*\.\.\/\.\.\/\.\.[\ w/-]*?\/redirect\?to=https?:\/\/placecats\.com\/(g\/)?[\d]+\/[\d]+.*/)` where `data` is fully attacker-controlled. The regex has unanchored `.*` at both ends and a lazy `[\ w/-]*?` in the middle. Node.js uses a backtracking regex engine. An attacker can send a crafted string such as `'../../../' + 'a/'.repeat(50000)` that causes catastrophic backtracking — the engine repeatedly tries combinations of the lazy quantifier against the fixed suffix `/redirect?to=...` that never appears. This blocks the JavaScript event loop thread for the duration of the match, causing all Socket.IO events to stall.
threats.evidence_summary: `data?.match(/.*\.\.\/\.\.\/\.\.[\ w/-]*?\/redirect\?to=.../)`  at line 47 applies an unanchored backtracking regex to an attacker-supplied string with no length or content pre-check.
threats.impact_description: A single crafted WebSocket message can block the Node.js event loop for seconds to minutes, causing a denial of service for all active connections.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no input length limit, no pre-validation before the regex match.
threats.mitigation_title: Pre-validate and length-cap data before regex match in verifySvgInjectionChallenge handler

steps: Add a length check before the regex: `if (typeof data !== 'string' || data.length > 500) return;` to reject oversized or non-string inputs.

Rewrite the regex to be anchored and non-backtracking: replace the leading `.*` with `^[^]*?` and reduce ambiguity in the middle quantifier, or use a non-backtracking approach by splitting on known delimiters first.
Consider using the Node.js `vm.runInNewContext()` with a timeout, or the `re2` package (a linear-time RE2 engine for Node), for any regex applied to untrusted input.

threats.title: Archived Action Dependency in Release Pipeline (release.yml)
threats.scenario: `release.yml:66` uses `dawidd6/action-get-tag@727a6f0a561be04e09013531e73a3983a65e3479` with a comment `# v1.1.0 TODO Action is archived and should be replaced eventually`. The action repository is archived, meaning it receives no security updates, bug fixes, or maintenance. While the SHA pin prevents tag-pointer attacks, the pinned code itself may contain vulnerabilities that will never be patched. This action runs in the release pipeline — the most privileged context in the repo (triggered by `push` to `v*` tags, producing artifacts uploaded to GitHub Releases and Docker images tagged with the version string). If the archived action's code has a vulnerability (e.g. insecure handling of the tag output, log injection via specially crafted tag names), it can be exploited to corrupt the release artifact naming or inject malicious content into `GITHUB_ENV`. The `TODO` comment acknowledges the risk without a remediation timeline.
threats.evidence_summary: `release.yml:66` declares a known-archived action with a `TODO` comment acknowledging it needs replacement, used in the highest-privilege release pipeline job.
threats.impact_description: Vulnerabilities in the unmaintained `dawidd6/action-get-tag` action in the release pipeline can corrupt artifact naming or inject values into `GITHUB_ENV` used by subsequent Docker push steps.
threats.controls_in_place: The action is SHA-pinned, preventing tag-pointer hijacking. The `TODO` comment acknowledges the maintenance gap.
threats.mitigation_title: Replace archived dawidd6/action-get-tag with a maintained alternative in release.yml

steps: Replace `dawidd6/action-get-tag` with the built-in `github.ref_name` context variable, which is available in GitHub Actions when the workflow is triggered by a tag push. This eliminates the external action dependency entirely: `echo "TAG_NAME=${{ github.ref_name }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV`.
Remove the `id: tag` step and update the subsequent `tags:` reference from `${{ steps.tag.outputs.tag }}` to `${{ github.ref_name }}` in the Docker build-push step.
remediation.code_example:
# release.yml — docker job, replace archived action with context variable
- name: "Get tag name"
  run: echo "TAG_NAME=${{ github.ref_name }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
# Then in build-push-action:
# tags: bkimminich/juice-shop:${{ env.TAG_NAME }}

threats.title: Dockerfile missing HEALTHCHECK instruction
threats.scenario: The Dockerfile has no HEALTHCHECK directive. Container orchestrators (Docker Compose, Kubernetes) cannot distinguish a hung or poisoned container from a healthy one, delaying incident detection and automated remediation.
threats.mitigation_title: Add HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1

threats.title: Data disclosure through cleartext transport
threats.scenario: Application data transmitted over HTTP without TLS encryption exposes sensitive information including credentials and session tokens to network interception. Attackers on the network path can perform passive eavesdropping or active man-in-the-middle attacks. CWE-319.
